title: OCCUPATIONAL BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE — Percutaneous NEEDLESTICK Injury in a 32-Year-Old Medical-Surgical RN from a Hollow-Bore IV Catheter While Disposing of It from a KNOWN HIV-POSITIVE Source Patient (Detectable Viral Load) Plus Unknown HBV/HCV Status: The Choreographed Immediate Response (WASH with Soap + Water, Do NOT Use Bleach/Alcohol/Peroxide, REPORT to Supervisor + Occupational/Employee Health Without Delay), OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard Compliance (Exposure Log + Confidentiality), Rapid SOURCE-Patient + EXPOSED-WORKER Baseline Serologies (HIV, HBV-HBsAg, HCV-Antibody), Pathogen-Specific POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS — HIV PEP Within HOURS (Ideally <2 h, No Later Than 72 h) with a 3-Drug Regimen (TDF + FTC + an INTEGRASE Inhibitor like Raltegravir/Dolutegravir) for 28 Days, HBV HBIG + Vaccine per Vaccination Status, HCV NO PEP but Monitor (HCV RNA at 3 Weeks + Antibody Follow-Up), Post-Exposure Counseling (Safer Sex + No Donation × 6 Months + Pregnancy/Breastfeeding Considerations), Serial Follow-Up Testing (HIV at 6 Weeks, 3 Months, 6 Months — Earlier with 4th-Generation Assays), Emotional + Peer Support, and PREVENTION (Engineered Safety Devices + NEVER RECAP + Sharps Containers at Point of Use + Standard Precautions)
subtitle: Occupational bloodborne pathogen (BBP) exposure is a common but high-stakes event: tens of thousands of percutaneous injuries occur annually among US healthcare workers, and the response in the first MINUTES TO HOURS shapes the outcome. Three pathogens dominate the calculus — HIV, HEPATITIS B (HBV), and HEPATITIS C (HCV) — and each has a different baseline risk per percutaneous exposure from a known-infected source (approximately 0.3% for HIV from a known-positive source, 6-30% for HBV from an HBeAg-positive source in an unvaccinated worker, and ~1.8% for HCV), a different prophylaxis pathway, and a different monitoring plan. IMMEDIATE bedside care for the exposed worker is straightforward: WASH the puncture site immediately with SOAP and WATER (mucous-membrane exposures with saline or clean water; eye exposures flush with copious water at an eye-wash station for 15 minutes); allow gentle bleeding under running water — do NOT vigorously squeeze the wound (no efficacy benefit and may cause additional tissue trauma); and do NOT apply BLEACH, ALCOHOL, HYDROGEN PEROXIDE, or other caustic agents (no benefit and tissue damage). REPORT the injury IMMEDIATELY to the charge nurse/supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or the Emergency Department after hours) — time is the most important variable for HIV PEP. The OSHA BLOODBORNE PATHOGENS STANDARD (29 CFR 1910.1030) requires annual training, free HBV vaccination, engineered safety devices, sharps containers at the point of use, an EXPOSURE INCIDENT REPORT, and CONFIDENTIALITY around the exposed worker and the source. SOURCE-PATIENT TESTING is performed per state law (most states permit testing for an occupational BBP exposure with informed consent; some allow without consent for occupational exposure) — rapid HIV, HBsAg, and anti-HCV are obtained where possible; ideally rapid 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody assays. EXPOSED-WORKER BASELINE labs include rapid HIV, HBV (anti-HBs to confirm immune status if vaccinated, HBsAg to exclude prior infection), anti-HCV, plus a pregnancy test if applicable. HIV PEP is TIME-CRITICAL — start WITHIN HOURS (ideally < 2 h, NO LATER than 72 h, with steeply declining efficacy after 72 h). The current US standard regimen is TENOFOVIR DISOPROXIL FUMARATE (TDF) + EMTRICITABINE (FTC) as a single tablet PLUS an INTEGRASE-STRAND-TRANSFER INHIBITOR — RALTEGRAVIR or DOLUTEGRAVIR — for 28 DAYS (4 weeks); continue if the source HIV status is confirmed positive (or unknown after risk assessment), discontinue if the source is confirmed negative on adequate testing. Counsel about expected side effects (GI upset, fatigue, headache) and arrange follow-up. HBV management depends on the EXPOSED WORKER'S vaccination + immune status and the SOURCE'S HBsAg status: a vaccinated worker with a documented anti-HBs of at least 10 mIU/mL needs no action; a vaccinated non-responder OR a worker with unknown response needs HEPATITIS B IMMUNE GLOBULIN (HBIG) plus a vaccine booster (and a revaccination series for known non-responders); an unvaccinated worker needs HBIG plus initiation of the HBV vaccine series — HBIG is ideally given WITHIN 24 HOURS and efficacy declines over the first 7 days. HCV currently has NO post-exposure prophylaxis available; the worker is monitored with anti-HCV + HCV RNA at baseline, HCV RNA at 3 weeks, and anti-HCV at 4-6 months (some institutions use HCV RNA at additional intervals). If the worker seroconverts to HCV, the standard treatment is a direct-acting antiviral (DAA) course with cure rates over 95%. FOLLOW-UP testing for HIV is at 6 WEEKS, 3 MONTHS, and 6 MONTHS (the 6-month time point can sometimes be omitted with a 4th-generation assay if all earlier results are negative). POST-EXPOSURE COUNSELING for the worker until cleared: use barrier protection (CONDOMS) for sexual activity; do NOT donate blood, tissue, plasma, organs, or sperm; pregnancy/breastfeeding counseling and obstetric/maternal-fetal medicine input if pregnant; symptoms of acute seroconversion (fever, fatigue, rash, lymphadenopathy, mononucleosis-like illness) to report. PEER and emotional SUPPORT are part of the standard — exposed workers commonly experience anxiety, sleep disturbance, and shame about the event; chaplaincy and employee assistance programs are offered, and confidentiality is preserved. PREVENTION is the most important long-term lesson: safety-ENGINEERED sharps devices (retractable, blunting, needleless systems), SHARPS CONTAINERS AT THE POINT OF USE, the NEVER-RECAP rule (recapping is the most common cause of needlestick), the SCOOP technique only if recapping is unavoidable (one-handed), passing sharps via a neutral zone rather than hand-to-hand, gloves and other PPE under standard precautions, and reinforcing a culture of immediate reporting without blame. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: using bleach/alcohol/peroxide on the wound; vigorously squeezing the wound; delaying or not reporting the exposure; delaying source-patient or exposed-worker testing; delaying HIV PEP beyond hours; missing the HBIG window or HBV vaccine algorithm based on serostatus; recapping needles; failing to maintain confidentiality. NURSING ROLE: deliver immediate bedside care (wash with soap and water, no caustics, no aggressive squeezing); REPORT immediately to supervisor and Occupational Health without delay; complete the OSHA exposure incident report; coordinate rapid source-patient and exposed-worker baseline serologies; ensure HIV PEP is started within hours of exposure as appropriate; apply the HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm correctly; monitor HCV; arrange serial follow-up testing and counseling; support the worker emotionally; and reinforce prevention (engineered devices + never recap + sharps containers at point of use + standard precautions).

patient.name: C. Reyes, RN (exposed worker)
patient.code: Not applicable — exposed healthcare worker scenario. Goal = immediate post-exposure response (wash with soap and water; no bleach/alcohol/peroxide; no aggressive squeezing); REPORT to supervisor + Occupational/Employee Health without delay; OSHA exposure incident report with confidentiality; rapid source-patient and exposed-worker baseline serologies (HIV/HBV-HBsAg/HCV-Ab); pathogen-specific PEP — HIV PEP within HOURS, HBV HBIG + vaccine per status, HCV monitor only; counseling (safer sex + no donation × 6 months + pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations); serial follow-up testing; emotional support; and prevention reinforcement
patient.admitDate: Mid-shift on a medical-surgical unit, the RN sustained a PERCUTANEOUS NEEDLESTICK from a hollow-bore IV catheter she was disposing of after removing it from a KNOWN HIV-POSITIVE patient with a DETECTABLE viral load (last HIV-1 RNA approximately 12,000 copies/mL; not on ART); the patient's HBV and HCV status are documented as PENDING/unknown. The exposed RN is otherwise healthy, fully HBV-vaccinated about a decade ago with no documented post-vaccine titer, not pregnant, on a daily prenatal multivitamin, and visibly distressed. The unit's needlestick policy and Occupational Health number are posted at the medication room. This case focuses on the choreographed response in the first MINUTES to HOURS — immediate bedside care, reporting, OSHA exposure incident report, source and exposed-worker baseline serologies, HIV PEP within hours, the HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm, HCV monitoring, counseling, follow-up testing, emotional support, and prevention.
patient.room: Med-surg unit; nearest sink and Occupational/Employee Health office; ED available after hours; HR/risk management notified per policy; confidentiality preserved

labs.n: PRESENTATION + immediate bedside care + OSHA reporting
labs.v: Mid-shift percutaneous needlestick on the left index finger from a hollow-bore IV catheter — the device was being disposed of after IV-catheter removal from a KNOWN HIV+ patient with detectable viral load (~12,000 copies/mL, not on ART); the source's HBV and HCV status are pending. The exposed RN is healthy, HBV-vaccinated about a decade ago without a documented post-vaccine titer, not pregnant. IMMEDIATE bedside care: WASH the site with SOAP and WATER under running water (mucous-membrane exposures with saline or clean water; eye exposure flush with copious water for 15 minutes at an eye-wash station); allow gentle bleeding — do NOT vigorously squeeze the wound; do NOT apply BLEACH, ALCOHOL, HYDROGEN PEROXIDE, or other caustic agents (no benefit, additional tissue trauma). REPORT IMMEDIATELY to charge nurse/supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or the Emergency Department after hours) — time is the most important variable for HIV PEP. The OSHA BLOODBORNE PATHOGENS STANDARD (29 CFR 1910.1030) requires annual training, free HBV vaccination, engineered safety devices, sharps containers at the point of use, an EXPOSURE INCIDENT REPORT (date/time/route/source identifiers/device/circumstances), and CONFIDENTIALITY around both the exposed worker and the source.
labs.r: Immediate bedside care: WASH w/ soap + water (mucous membranes saline/clean water; eyes 15-min flush); allow gentle bleeding — DON'T vigorously squeeze; NO bleach/alcohol/peroxide. REPORT immediately to supervisor + Occ/Employee Health (or ED after hours). OSHA BBP Standard 29 CFR 1910.1030 → annual training + free HBV vaccine + engineered safety devices + sharps containers at point of use + EXPOSURE INCIDENT REPORT (date/time/route/source/device/circumstances) + CONFIDENTIALITY (worker + source). RN role: choreographed immediate response.

labs.n: Source + exposed-worker baseline testing + HIV POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS (PEP within HOURS)
labs.v: SOURCE-PATIENT testing is performed per state law (most states permit testing for occupational BBP exposure with informed consent; some permit without consent for occupational exposure) — rapid HIV, HBsAg, anti-HCV are obtained where possible (ideally 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody). EXPOSED-WORKER BASELINE labs: rapid HIV; HBV (anti-HBs to confirm immune status if vaccinated; HBsAg to exclude prior infection); anti-HCV; pregnancy test if applicable. HIV PEP is TIME-CRITICAL — start WITHIN HOURS (ideally < 2 h, NO LATER than 72 h; efficacy declines steeply after 72 h). The current US standard regimen is TENOFOVIR DISOPROXIL FUMARATE (TDF) + EMTRICITABINE (FTC) as a single tablet PLUS an INTEGRASE-STRAND-TRANSFER INHIBITOR — RALTEGRAVIR or DOLUTEGRAVIR — for 28 DAYS (4 weeks); continue if source is confirmed positive (or unknown after risk assessment), discontinue if source is confirmed negative on adequate testing. Counsel on adherence + expected side effects (GI upset, fatigue, headache) and arrange follow-up. Baseline risk per percutaneous from a known HIV+ source is approximately 0.3% (1/333); risk rises with deep injury, hollow-bore device with visible blood, terminal-illness source, or high viral load.
labs.r: SOURCE testing per state law/consent (rapid HIV/HBsAg/anti-HCV; ideally 4th-gen Ag/Ab). EXPOSED-WORKER baseline: rapid HIV + anti-HBs (if vaccinated) + HBsAg + anti-HCV + pregnancy test if applicable. HIV PEP = WITHIN HOURS (ideally <2 h; NO LATER than 72 h; efficacy declines steeply after 72 h). US standard regimen: TDF + FTC + INTEGRASE INHIBITOR (raltegravir or dolutegravir) x 28 DAYS; continue if source HIV+ confirmed, discontinue if source HIV- on adequate testing. Baseline risk ~0.3%/percutaneous from a known HIV+ source. RN role: rapid baseline labs + PEP within hours.

labs.n: HBV (HBIG + vaccine algorithm by status) + HCV (no PEP — monitor) + counseling + follow-up testing
labs.v: HBV: management depends on the EXPOSED WORKER'S vaccination + immune status and the SOURCE'S HBsAg status. (a) Vaccinated worker with a documented anti-HBs >= 10 mIU/mL — no action. (b) Vaccinated NON-RESPONDER OR worker with unknown response — HEPATITIS B IMMUNE GLOBULIN (HBIG) plus a vaccine BOOSTER and a repeat anti-HBs titer; for a known non-responder a SECOND HBIG dose at 1 month and revaccination are often appropriate. (c) UNVACCINATED worker — HBIG plus initiation of the HBV vaccine series. HBIG is ideally given WITHIN 24 HOURS; efficacy declines over the first 7 days. Baseline risk per percutaneous from an HBeAg-positive source is approximately 6-30% in an unvaccinated worker — far higher than HIV. HCV: NO post-exposure prophylaxis is available. The worker is monitored with baseline anti-HCV and HCV RNA (where indicated), HCV RNA at 3 weeks, and anti-HCV at 4-6 months (some institutions add HCV RNA at additional intervals). If the worker seroconverts to HCV, treatment with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) achieves cure in over 95%. COUNSELING: until cleared, use barrier protection (CONDOMS) for sexual activity, do NOT donate blood/tissue/plasma/organs/sperm, address pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations and obstetric input if pregnant, and report acute-seroconversion symptoms (fever, fatigue, rash, lymphadenopathy, mononucleosis-like illness). HIV FOLLOW-UP testing is at 6 WEEKS, 3 MONTHS, and 6 MONTHS (the 6-month time point may be omitted with a 4th-generation assay if earlier results are negative). PEER/EMOTIONAL support is the standard — exposed workers commonly experience anxiety, sleep disturbance, and shame; chaplaincy and Employee Assistance Programs are offered and confidentiality is preserved.
labs.r: HBV: (a) vaccinated w/ anti-HBs ≥10 → no action; (b) vaccinated non-responder/unknown response → HBIG + booster + titer (known non-responder = second HBIG at 1 mo + revaccinate); (c) unvaccinated → HBIG + start vaccine series; HBIG ideally <24 h, efficacy ↓ over 7 days. Baseline percutaneous risk from HBeAg+ source ~6-30% in unvaccinated. HCV: NO PEP; monitor anti-HCV + HCV RNA at baseline, HCV RNA at 3 wk, anti-HCV at 4-6 mo; seroconversion → DAA (cure >95%). COUNSELING: condoms + NO donation × 6 mo + pregnancy/breastfeeding + acute seroconversion sx to report. HIV follow-up at 6 wk + 3 mo + 6 mo (6 mo may be omitted w/ 4th-gen assay). Peer/EAP + chaplaincy + confidentiality.

labs.v: PREVENTION is the most important long-term lesson. Use safety-ENGINEERED sharps devices (retractable, blunting, needleless systems) per OSHA; place SHARPS CONTAINERS at the POINT OF USE; observe the NEVER-RECAP rule (recapping is the most common cause of needlestick); if recapping is genuinely unavoidable, use the ONE-HANDED SCOOP technique; pass sharps via a NEUTRAL ZONE rather than hand-to-hand in procedures; wear gloves and other PPE under standard precautions; do NOT walk around with an uncapped sharp; activate safety mechanisms after each use; reinforce a JUST-CULTURE of immediate reporting without blame; participate in annual training. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the nurse did NOT apply bleach/alcohol/hydrogen peroxide or other caustics to the wound; did NOT vigorously squeeze the wound; did NOT delay reporting or skip the OSHA exposure incident report; did NOT wait beyond hours to consider HIV PEP; did NOT miss the HBIG/vaccine algorithm step based on serostatus; did NOT continue working without arranging coverage and emotional support; did NOT recap the needle or carry an uncapped sharp; and did NOT compromise the exposed worker's or source's confidentiality. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: immediate bedside wash and clean dressing; prompt report to supervisor + Occupational Health; OSHA exposure incident report with confidentiality; rapid source + exposed-worker baseline serologies; HIV PEP started within hours with a current 3-drug regimen; HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm applied correctly to serostatus; HCV baseline + follow-up testing; comprehensive counseling (safer sex, no donation, pregnancy/breastfeeding, acute seroconversion); peer/EAP/chaplaincy support; serial follow-up testing scheduled; and unit prevention reinforcement (engineered devices, never recap, sharps containers at point of use, standard precautions). EXPECTED outcome: the exposed worker is supported, follow-up testing is completed, and the unit's prevention culture is strengthened.
labs.r: PREVENTION: engineered safety devices + sharps containers at point of use + NEVER RECAP (one-handed scoop if unavoidable) + neutral-zone sharps passing + PPE under standard precautions + activate safety mechanism after use + just-culture reporting + annual training. Negatives confirm correct practice (no bleach/alcohol/peroxide, no vigorous squeezing, no delayed report/OSHA, no delayed HIV PEP, no missed HBV algorithm, no continuing work w/o coverage + support, no recapping, no confidentiality breach). Protective: bedside wash + prompt report + OSHA log + rapid baseline labs + HIV PEP within hours + HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm + HCV monitor + counseling + peer/EAP/chaplaincy + serial follow-up + prevention reinforcement.

labs.v: 12,000 copies/mL (detectable; not on ART; raises percutaneous transmission risk and supports timely HIV PEP)
Pending (no documented prior titer despite remote completed vaccine series — triggers HBIG + booster pathway if < 10 mIU/mL)
Negative (baseline; follow-up anti-HCV at 4-6 months and HCV RNA at 3 weeks)

nursesNote: Med-surg unit, mid-shift — percutaneous NEEDLESTICK to left index finger from a hollow-bore IV catheter being disposed of after IV catheter removal from a KNOWN HIV+ source (viral load 12,000 copies/mL, not on ART); source HBV/HCV pending. Exposed RN: healthy, HBV-vaccinated ~10 y ago w/o documented titer, not pregnant. IMMEDIATE bedside care: WASHED w/ soap + water under running water; allowed to bleed gently — did NOT vigorously squeeze; NO bleach/alcohol/peroxide applied; clean dressing. Charge RN notified; patient coverage arranged. REPORT to supervisor + Occupational/Employee Health within minutes (would go to ED after hours); OSHA EXPOSURE INCIDENT REPORT completed (date/time/route/source/device/circumstances); confidentiality preserved (worker + source); risk management notified per policy. SOURCE testing per consent (rapid HIV confirmed positive; HBsAg + anti-HCV in process). EXPOSED-WORKER baseline: rapid 4th-gen HIV Ag/Ab NEG; anti-HBs pending (no documented titer); HBsAg + anti-HCV NEG; pregnancy test NEG. HIV PEP STARTED within hours (TDF + FTC + integrase inhibitor [raltegravir or dolutegravir]) x 28 days; counseled on adherence + side effects (GI upset/fatigue/headache). HBV algorithm: if anti-HBs < 10 mIU/mL → HBIG within 24 h + revaccinate; if ≥ 10 → no action. HCV: NO PEP — baseline negative, plan HCV RNA at 3 wk + anti-HCV at 4-6 mo. COUNSELING: safer sex (condoms) + NO blood/tissue/organ/sperm donation × 6 mo + pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations + acute-seroconversion sx to report (fever/fatigue/rash/LAD/mono-like). Chaplaincy + EAP offered. SERIAL HIV testing scheduled at 6 wk + 3 mo + 6 mo (6 mo may be omitted w/ 4th-gen assay if earlier negative). PREVENTION reinforced at unit huddle (engineered safety devices + NEVER RECAP + sharps containers at point of use + activate safety mechanism + neutral-zone sharps passing + standard precautions + just-culture reporting). Confidentiality preserved throughout. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT apply bleach/alcohol/peroxide to wound, did NOT vigorously squeeze, did NOT delay report or OSHA log, did NOT wait beyond hours for HIV PEP decision, did NOT miss HBV algorithm step based on serostatus, did NOT continue working w/o coverage + support, did NOT recap the needle, did NOT compromise confidentiality.
providerOrders: OCCUPATIONAL BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE: percutaneous needlestick from a hollow-bore IV catheter, source known HIV+ with detectable viral load; HBV/HCV source status pending

IMMEDIATE BEDSIDE: wash the puncture site with soap and water under running water; allow gentle bleeding; do NOT vigorously squeeze the wound; do NOT apply bleach/alcohol/hydrogen peroxide or other caustic agents; clean dressing

REPORTING: report immediately to charge nurse/supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or Emergency Department after hours); complete OSHA exposure incident report (date/time/route/source/device/circumstances) with confidentiality maintained for the exposed worker and source
SOURCE-PATIENT TESTING: obtain consent per state law; rapid HIV antigen/antibody, HBsAg, anti-HCV; document and communicate results per protocol
EXPOSED-WORKER BASELINE LABS: rapid 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody; anti-HBs (and HBsAg as indicated); anti-HCV; pregnancy test if applicable

HIV POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS (PEP): initiate within hours of exposure (ideally < 2 h, no later than 72 h); 3-drug regimen for 28 days — tenofovir disoproxil fumarate + emtricitabine plus an integrase-strand-transfer inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir); continue if source HIV-positive (or unknown after risk assessment), discontinue if source HIV-negative on adequate testing; counsel on adherence and side effects

HBV MANAGEMENT (by status): vaccinated worker with anti-HBs >= 10 mIU/mL — no action; vaccinated non-responder OR unknown response — HBIG + vaccine booster (consider second HBIG and revaccination per protocol if known non-responder); unvaccinated — HBIG + initiate vaccine series; HBIG ideally within 24 hours (efficacy declines over 7 days)
HCV MANAGEMENT: no PEP available; baseline anti-HCV (and HCV RNA as indicated); HCV RNA at 3 weeks; anti-HCV at 4-6 months; if seroconverts, refer for direct-acting antiviral therapy

COUNSELING: barrier protection (condoms) for sexual activity, no blood/tissue/plasma/organ/sperm donation, address pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, report acute seroconversion symptoms (fever, fatigue, rash, lymphadenopathy, mononucleosis-like illness); chaplaincy and Employee Assistance Program

FOLLOW-UP TESTING: HIV at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months (the 6-month time point may be omitted with a 4th-generation assay if earlier results are negative); HCV per schedule above; HBV per algorithm

PREVENTION/REINFORCEMENT: engineered safety devices; sharps containers at point of use; never recap (one-handed scoop only if unavoidable); neutral-zone sharps passing during procedures; activate safety mechanism after each use; PPE under standard precautions; just-culture reporting; annual OSHA training

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this occupational needlestick?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Percutaneous needlestick from a hollow-bore IV catheter just used on a KNOWN HIV+ source with a detectable viral load — high-risk exposure that requires the choreographed response in MINUTES TO HOURS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — this is a high-risk exposure; time is the most important variable. RN role: recognize + respond.

opts.text: Immediate bedside care: WASH with SOAP and WATER under running water; allow gentle bleeding; do NOT vigorously squeeze; do NOT apply bleach/alcohol/hydrogen peroxide
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct first-aid; caustic agents cause tissue damage without benefit. RN role: bedside care.

opts.text: Report IMMEDIATELY to supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or ED after hours); complete the OSHA exposure incident report; preserve confidentiality for the exposed worker and source
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — reporting drives PEP timing and OSHA compliance. RN role: report + log + confidentiality.

opts.text: Obtain rapid SOURCE and EXPOSED-WORKER baseline serologies (HIV, HBV-HBsAg, HCV-Ab; anti-HBs to confirm immune status; pregnancy test if applicable)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — baseline testing guides PEP and follow-up. RN role: baseline labs.

opts.text: HIV PEP is TIME-CRITICAL — start within HOURS (ideally < 2 h, no later than 72 h); standard 3-drug regimen TDF + FTC + integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir) for 28 days
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — efficacy declines after 72 h; do not wait. RN role: HIV PEP within hours.

opts.text: HBV management is by EXPOSED-WORKER status (vaccinated responder = no action; non-responder or unknown = HBIG + booster; unvaccinated = HBIG + start series — HBIG within 24 h); HCV has NO PEP — monitor (anti-HCV + HCV RNA per schedule)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pathogen-specific pathways. RN role: apply correct HBV algorithm + HCV monitoring.

opts.text: Apply BLEACH or HYDROGEN PEROXIDE to the wound and vigorously squeeze to 'force the blood out'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Caustics damage tissue and vigorous squeezing has no benefit. Wash with soap and water; allow gentle bleeding.

opts.text: Delay reporting and finish the shift first; consider HIV PEP only the next day if she still has symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Delayed reporting and delayed HIV PEP markedly reduce efficacy; report immediately and start PEP within hours.

opts.text: Baseline risk per percutaneous exposure is approximately 0.3% for HIV from a known-positive source, 6-30% for HBV from an HBeAg-positive source in an unvaccinated worker, and approximately 1.8% for HCV
opts.rat: CORRECT — relative-risk principle. Foundation for prioritizing PEP urgency.

opts.text: Immediate bedside care is to wash with soap and water; mucous membranes with saline or clean water; eyes with copious water at an eye-wash station for ~15 minutes — never bleach, alcohol, or peroxide
opts.rat: CORRECT — first-aid principle. Foundation for safe initial care.

opts.text: OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard (29 CFR 1910.1030) requires annual training, free HBV vaccination, engineered safety devices, sharps containers at the point of use, an exposure incident report, and confidentiality
opts.rat: CORRECT — regulatory principle. Foundation for compliant response.

opts.text: HIV PEP is most effective when started WITHIN HOURS of exposure (ideally < 2 h) and is not recommended after 72 h; the US standard is TDF + FTC + an integrase inhibitor for 28 days
opts.rat: CORRECT — PEP principle. Foundation for time-critical action.

opts.text: HBV decisions depend on the exposed worker's vaccination/immune status and the source's HBsAg status; HBIG is most effective within 24 hours, with efficacy declining over the first 7 days
opts.rat: CORRECT — HBV principle. Foundation for the correct algorithm.

opts.text: HCV has no post-exposure prophylaxis; the worker is monitored with anti-HCV at baseline and 4-6 months and HCV RNA at 3 weeks; seroconversion is treated with direct-acting antivirals with cure rates over 95%
opts.rat: CORRECT — HCV principle. Foundation for correct monitoring.

opts.text: Applying bleach or hydrogen peroxide to the wound improves outcomes and reduces seroconversion
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. No benefit and tissue damage; wash with soap and water and allow gentle bleeding.

opts.text: Recapping needles two-handed after each use is the safest practice and is recommended by OSHA
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Recapping is the most common cause of needlestick; the rule is NEVER RECAP (one-handed scoop only if unavoidable).

opts.text: Immediate bedside care — wash with soap and water under running water; allow gentle bleeding; clean dressing; no caustics; arrange coverage for her patients
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The first-aid response is the only thing only the worker can do right now. RN role: bedside care.

opts.text: Report immediately to supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health; complete the OSHA exposure incident report with confidentiality maintained
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Reporting drives PEP timing and OSHA compliance. RN role: report + log.

opts.text: Obtain rapid source-patient and exposed-worker baseline serologies (HIV, HBV anti-HBs/HBsAg, HCV); pregnancy test if applicable; start HIV PEP within hours as appropriate
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Time-critical baseline + PEP. RN role: baseline labs + HIV PEP.

opts.text: Apply the HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm to status; arrange HCV baseline + monitoring; counsel on safer sex + no donation + pregnancy/breastfeeding + acute-seroconversion symptoms
opts.rat: #4 — Pathogen-specific management + counseling. RN role: HBV algorithm + HCV monitor + counseling.

opts.text: Arrange serial HIV follow-up testing (6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months) and HCV follow-up; provide emotional support (chaplaincy + EAP) and reinforce prevention (engineered devices + never recap + sharps containers at point of use + standard precautions)
opts.rat: #5 — Follow-up + support + prevention sustain the response. RN role: follow-up + support + prevention.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this exposure.

opts.text: Wash the puncture site immediately with soap and water under running water; allow gentle bleeding; apply a clean dressing; arrange coverage for her patients
opts.rat: Correct first-aid. RN role: bedside care.

opts.text: Report immediately to supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or ED after hours); complete the OSHA exposure incident report with confidentiality maintained
opts.rat: Drives PEP timing and OSHA compliance. RN role: report + log.

opts.text: Obtain rapid source-patient and exposed-worker baseline serologies (HIV, HBV anti-HBs/HBsAg, HCV-Ab); pregnancy test if applicable
opts.rat: Time-critical baseline. RN role: baseline labs.

opts.text: Start HIV PEP within hours of exposure with TDF + FTC + an integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir) for 28 days; continue if source HIV-positive (or unknown after risk assessment), discontinue if source HIV-negative
opts.rat: Time-critical and protocol-driven. RN role: HIV PEP.

opts.text: Apply the HBV algorithm to the exposed worker's vaccination/immune status (HBIG + booster for non-responder or unknown response; HBIG + initiate vaccine series if unvaccinated); HBIG ideally within 24 hours
opts.rat: Correct HBV pathway. RN role: HBV algorithm.

opts.text: Monitor HCV with baseline anti-HCV (and HCV RNA as indicated), HCV RNA at 3 weeks, and anti-HCV at 4-6 months; treat seroconversion with direct-acting antivirals
opts.rat: Correct HCV monitoring without PEP. RN role: HCV monitor.

opts.text: Counsel safer sex (condoms), no blood/tissue/plasma/organ/sperm donation, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, and acute-seroconversion symptoms; provide chaplaincy/EAP/peer support
opts.rat: Comprehensive counseling. RN role: counseling + support.

opts.text: Apply bleach or hydrogen peroxide to the wound and vigorously squeeze to force out the blood
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — caustic damage with no benefit; wash with soap and water and allow gentle bleeding.

opts.text: Delay reporting and the OSHA exposure incident report until the end of the shift; consider HIV PEP the next day if she still has symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — delayed reporting and delayed HIV PEP markedly reduce efficacy and violate the standard.

opts.text: Recap the contaminated needle two-handed before discarding it and disclose the exposed worker's name + the source's HIV status at unit huddle to 'inform everyone'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — recapping is the leading cause of needlesticks; never recap; confidentiality for the exposed worker AND the source is required.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Immediate bedside care — wash with soap and water under running water; allow gentle bleeding; clean dressing; arrange coverage for her patients
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The only thing only the worker can do right now. RN role: bedside care.

opts.text: Report immediately to supervisor and Occupational/Employee Health (or ED after hours); complete the OSHA exposure incident report with confidentiality maintained
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Drives PEP timing and OSHA compliance. RN role: report + log.

opts.text: Obtain rapid source-patient and exposed-worker baseline serologies and start HIV PEP within hours as appropriate
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Time-critical baseline + PEP. RN role: baseline + HIV PEP.

opts.text: Apply HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm to status; arrange HCV monitoring; counsel on safer sex + no donation + pregnancy/breastfeeding + acute-seroconversion symptoms; chaplaincy/EAP offered
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Pathogen-specific management + counseling + support. RN role: HBV + HCV + counseling.

opts.text: Arrange serial HIV follow-up (6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months) and HCV follow-up; reinforce prevention (engineered devices + never recap + sharps containers at point of use + standard precautions); preserve confidentiality throughout
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Follow-up + prevention + confidentiality complete the response. RN role: follow-up + prevention + confidentiality.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The RN washed the site with soap and water under running water, allowed gentle bleeding, applied a clean dressing, reported immediately, and the OSHA exposure incident report was completed with confidentiality maintained
opts.rat: Correct first-aid + prompt reporting + OSHA compliance. Model practice.

opts.text: Rapid baseline serologies were obtained and HIV PEP (TDF + FTC + integrase inhibitor) was started within hours of exposure
opts.rat: Time-critical action consistent with the standard. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: The HBV HBIG + vaccine algorithm was applied to her vaccination/immune status; HCV baseline and follow-up monitoring were arranged; counseling and EAP/chaplaincy support were provided
opts.rat: Pathogen-specific pathways + counseling + support. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Serial HIV follow-up at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months was arranged; the unit reinforced prevention (engineered devices, never-recap, sharps containers at point of use, standard precautions); confidentiality was preserved
opts.rat: Follow-up + prevention + confidentiality. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse applied bleach to the wound and vigorously squeezed it to 'force out the blood'; tissue damage worsened and PEP timing was delayed
opts.rat: ⚠️ Caustics + aggressive squeezing have no benefit and harm tissue. Wash with soap and water and allow gentle bleeding.

opts.text: Alternative — the exposure was not reported until the next day and HIV PEP was started only at 48 hours; the efficacy window was missed
opts.rat: ⚠️ Delayed reporting and delayed HIV PEP markedly reduce efficacy and violate the standard.

opts.text: Alternative — the needle was recapped two-handed before discarding, and the exposed worker's name plus the source's HIV status were announced at unit huddle to 'inform everyone'
opts.rat: ⚠️ Recapping is the leading cause of needlesticks (never recap); confidentiality must be maintained for both the exposed worker and the source.

opts.text: Alternative — the HBV algorithm was skipped because the worker said 'I had the vaccine a long time ago,' without checking anti-HBs or considering HBIG/booster
opts.rat: ⚠️ The algorithm requires anti-HBs documentation (or assessment if undocumented); unknown response or non-responder triggers HBIG + booster.

opts.text: Alternative — no counseling on safer sex, donation deferral, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, or acute-seroconversion symptoms was given; the worker received no peer/EAP support
opts.rat: ⚠️ Counseling + support are part of the standard response; missing them risks transmission to others and harms the worker.

opts.text: At 6 months her HIV, HBV, and HCV testing remain negative; she returned to work using engineered safety devices, never recapping, and discarding sharps at the point of use
opts.rat: Sustained correct practice + completed follow-up. Ideal outcome.
